section.title: The Resurrection and Thomas
section.sectionRationale: The dawn of the new creation. Mary finds the tomb empty, the beloved disciple sees and believes, and the risen Jesus reveals Himself to Mary in the garden, calling her by name. That evening He appears to the disciples, gives them peace and the Spirit, and sends them on mission. A week later He meets doubting Thomas, who confesses Him Lord and God. The chapter closes with John's own statement of why he wrote: that we may believe and have life.
section.thesis: The risen Jesus turns grief into joy and doubt into worship, appearing alive to Mary, to the gathered disciples whom He sends with peace and the Spirit, and to Thomas, who confesses Him Lord and God, and the whole Gospel is written so that readers may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and by believing have life in His name.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the empty tomb and the beloved disciple's belief; the risen Jesus and Mary in the garden; the appearance to the disciples with peace, mission, and the Spirit; the confession of Thomas; and the stated purpose of the Gospel.

verses.text: Early on the first day of the week, while it was still dark, Mary Magdalene went to the tomb and saw that the stone had been removed from the entrance.
So she came running to Simon Peter and the other disciple, the one whom Jesus loved. “They have taken the Lord out of the tomb,” she said, “and we do not know where they have put Him!”
Then Peter and the other disciple set out for the tomb.
The two were running together, but the other disciple outran Peter and reached the tomb first.
He bent down and looked in at the linen cloths lying there, but he did not go in.
Simon Peter arrived just after him. He entered the tomb and saw the linen cloths lying there.
The cloth that had been around Jesus’ head was rolled up, lying separate from the linen cloths.
Then the other disciple, who had reached the tomb first, also went in. And he saw and believed.
For they still did not understand from the Scripture that Jesus had to rise from the dead.
Then the disciples returned to their homes.
But Mary stood outside the tomb weeping. And as she wept, she bent down to look into the tomb,
and she saw two angels in white sitting where the body of Jesus had lain, one at the head and the other at the feet.
“Woman, why are you weeping?” they asked. “Because they have taken my Lord away,” she said, “and I do not know where they have put Him.”
When she had said this, she turned around and saw Jesus standing there. But she did not recognize that it was Jesus.
“Woman, why are you weeping?” Jesus asked. “Whom are you seeking?” Thinking He was the gardener, she said, “Sir, if you have carried Him off, tell me where you have put Him, and I will get Him.”
Jesus said to her, “Mary.” She turned and said to Him in Hebrew, “Rabboni!” (which means “Teacher”).
“Do not cling to Me,” Jesus said, “for I have not yet ascended to the Father. But go and tell My brothers, ‘I am ascending to My Father and your Father, to My God and your God.’”
Mary Magdalene went and announced to the disciples, “I have seen the Lord!” And she told them what He had said to her.

It was the first day of the week, and that very evening, while the disciples were together with the doors locked for fear of the Jews, Jesus came and stood among them. “Peace be with you!” He said to them.
After He had said this, He showed them His hands and His side. The disciples rejoiced when they saw the Lord.
Again Jesus said to them, “Peace be with you. As the Father has sent Me, so also I am sending you.”
When He had said this, He breathed on them and said, “Receive the Holy {{pneuma|Spirit}}.
If you forgive anyone his sins, they are forgiven; if you withhold forgiveness from anyone, it is withheld.”
Now Thomas called Didymus, one of the Twelve, was not with the disciples when Jesus came.

So the other disciples told him, “We have seen the Lord!” But he replied, “Unless I see the nail marks in His hands, and put my finger where the nails have been, and put my hand into His side, I will never believe.”
Eight days later, His disciples were once again inside with the doors locked, and Thomas was with them. Jesus came and stood among them and said, “Peace be with you.”
Then Jesus said to Thomas, “Put your finger here and look at My hands. Reach out your hand and put it into My side. Stop doubting and believe.”
Thomas replied, “My Lord and my {{theos|God}}!”
Jesus said to him, “Because you have seen Me, you have believed; blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.”

units.label: The purpose of John's book
units.note: that you may believe and have life

verses.text: Jesus performed many other signs in the presence of His disciples, which are not written in this book.
But these are written so that you may {{pisteuo|believe}} that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have {{zoe|life}} in His name.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, breath. The risen Jesus breathes on the disciples and says, Receive the Holy Spirit, the same word for breath and Spirit. The act recalls God breathing life into Adam and points to the new creation now begun; the Spirit long promised in the farewell discourse is given to empower the sent disciples for their mission of proclaiming forgiveness.

theos.gloss: God. Thomas's cry, my Lord and my God, is the climactic confession of the Gospel, addressing the risen Jesus with the full weight of deity. It answers the prologue, the Word was God, forming a frame around the whole book: what John declared at the start, Thomas now confesses face to face before the risen Christ.

pisteuo.gloss: To believe, trust. The purpose statement names belief as the Gospel's aim: these things are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God. Belief here is not bare assent but trust that receives Christ and clings to Him, the very response the whole book has been urging from its first chapter.

zoe.gloss: Life, eternal life. The final purpose of believing is that you may have life in His name. This life is the gift woven through the whole Gospel, from the life that was the light of men in the prologue to the resurrection life secured here; it is received now by faith and is the very reason John wrote.

basic.context: On the first day of the week Mary Magdalene finds the stone rolled away and runs to tell Peter and John, who race to the tomb and find it empty, the grave clothes lying there. John sees and believes. Mary, weeping, meets the risen Jesus, whom she mistakes for the gardener until He speaks her name. That evening Jesus appears to the frightened disciples behind locked doors, showing His wounds, giving them peace, sending them as the Father sent Him, and breathing on them the Spirit. Thomas, absent, refuses to believe until, a week later, Jesus meets him and Thomas cries, My Lord and my God. John then states why he wrote it all: that we may believe and have life.
basic.hermeneutics: The resurrection is the turning point of the whole story, and John gives it as eyewitness testimony. The key questions are, is Jesus truly risen, and how should we respond. The signs of the empty tomb and the appearances lead to belief; Thomas's confession, my Lord and my God, is the response the whole Gospel aims for. Note the tender personal touch, Jesus calling Mary by name, and the blessing on those who believe without seeing, which includes every later reader. The purpose statement in verse 31 is the key to the entire book.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter climbs to two great words. Jesus breathes the pneuma, the Spirit, on the disciples, echoing the breath of new creation. Thomas confesses Him theos, God, the Gospel's highest confession. And John states his purpose: that you may pisteuo, believe, and have zoe, life, in His name.
basic.typology: Jesus breathing the Spirit recalls God breathing life into Adam, marking the dawn of new creation. The garden of the resurrection answers the garden of Eden, the risen Christ mistaken for the gardener being the true gardener of the new creation. The first day of the week begins a new creation week.
basic.christ: Christ is risen, the conqueror of death, who turns grief to joy and doubt to worship. He is Lord and God, confessed by Thomas, the fulfillment of the prologue. He sends His people as the Father sent Him and gives them the Spirit.
basic.god: God the Father raised the Son and is now the Father of all who believe, for the risen Jesus calls the disciples His brothers and speaks of My Father and your Father. The resurrection vindicates the Son and opens the way into the family of God.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 2 tells of God breathing life into Adam. John 1 declared the Word was God. Romans 1 says Jesus was declared Son of God by the resurrection. 1 Peter 1 speaks of the living hope through His rising.
basic.oneStory: On the first day of a new creation week, the risen Jesus steps from the tomb as the firstborn of the new creation, turning mourning into joy. He gathers and sends His people, breathes on them the Spirit, and draws even the doubter to confess Him Lord and God, so that the whole Gospel's aim is reached: that we may believe and have life in His name.

context.literary: The resurrection narrative and the original conclusion of the Gospel, culminating in Thomas's confession and the purpose statement. It is structured by a series of encounters, each moving from misunderstanding or grief to recognition and faith, mirroring the reader's own intended journey. Verses 30 and 31 form the climax of the whole book, stating explicitly why John wrote, and framing the Gospel with the prologue's confession of Christ's deity.
context.historical: Burial cloths and a sealed tomb, the testimony of women as first witnesses, a detail unlikely to be invented in that culture, and the disciples' fear behind locked doors all lend historical texture. The first day of the week became the Christians' day of worship, the Lord's Day, because of the resurrection. The eight days later reflects Jewish inclusive counting, the following Sunday. Thomas's demand for physical proof reflects the concrete, bodily nature of the resurrection claim.
context.canonical: The resurrection is the ground of the New Testament's whole proclamation, Romans 1:4 and 1 Corinthians 15. Jesus breathing the Spirit anticipates and is often related to Pentecost, Acts 2, and recalls Genesis 2:7 and Ezekiel 37:9. Thomas's my Lord and my God forms an inclusion with John 1:1, the Word was God, and stands among the New Testament's clearest confessions of Christ's deity. The purpose statement defines the Gospel's evangelistic aim, echoed in 1 John 5:13.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To testify that Jesus rose bodily from the dead, to trace the movement from grief and doubt to faith and worship, climaxing in Thomas's confession of Christ as Lord and God, and to state plainly that the Gospel was written so that readers may believe and have life in His name.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the historical resurrection appearances. Its abiding truths are that Jesus is risen and is Lord and God, that His people are sent on mission with the Spirit, and that belief in Him brings life; the blessing on those who believe without seeing and the purpose statement address every later reader directly.

debates.question: What is the significance of Jesus breathing on the disciples and saying, Receive the Holy Spirit?

views.label: A symbolic anticipation of Pentecost
views.case: Since the full outpouring comes at Pentecost in Acts 2, many read this as a prophetic sign-act, a foretaste and pledge of the Spirit soon to be given in power for the church's mission.

views.label: A real impartation for the new-creation community
views.case: The deliberate echo of God breathing life into Adam suggests a genuine giving of resurrection life and the Spirit to the disciples as the new-creation people, with Pentecost as its public empowering.

debates.bearing: Both affirm that the risen Christ is the giver of the Spirit and that His people are constituted and sent by that gift, whether this moment is chiefly anticipatory or a real first bestowal completed at Pentecost.

debates.question: How should Thomas be understood, and what is the point of the blessing in verse 29?

views.label: Honest doubt met by grace
views.case: Thomas is not uniquely faithless; he asks only what the others already received, sight of the risen Lord, and Jesus graciously meets him, drawing out the Gospel's highest confession.

views.label: A pointer to future believers
views.case: The blessing on those who have not seen and yet believe reaches past Thomas to every later reader, commending the faith that rests on the apostolic testimony now written down.

debates.bearing: Together they encourage honest doubters that Christ meets them, while directing all who come after to a faith founded on the reliable eyewitness testimony recorded so that they may believe.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter rises to its two great climaxes. The risen Jesus breathes on the disciples and says, receive the Holy pneuma, the Spirit, using the word that also means breath, a deliberate echo of God breathing life into Adam and a sign of the new creation begun. Then Thomas, confronted by the wounds of the risen Christ, cries, my Lord and my theos, my God, the highest confession in the Gospel, answering the prologue's declaration that the Word was God. And John states his whole purpose: these things are written so that you may pisteuo, believe, that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have zoe, life, in His name.

typology.type: God breathing life into Adam, Genesis 2:7
typology.fulfillment: The risen Jesus breathing the Spirit on His disciples, the new-creation people, John 20:22.
The garden of the resurrection, where the risen Christ, mistaken for the gardener, begins the new creation, John 20:15.

typology.type: The first day and a new creation week
typology.fulfillment: The resurrection on the first day of the week, inaugurating the new creation, John 20:1 and 19.

study.christ: Christ is risen, bodily and victorious over death, the same Jesus, still bearing the wounds, yet gloriously alive. He turns Mary's grief to joy by calling her name, transforms the disciples' fear into rejoicing, and draws the doubting Thomas to the Gospel's highest confession, my Lord and my God. He is Lord and God, the Word made flesh now risen, who sends His people as the Father sent Him and breathes on them the Spirit. He pronounces a blessing on all who will believe without seeing, gathering every future reader into the joy of resurrection faith and life.
study.god: God the Father has raised the Son, vindicating Him and all He claimed, and through the resurrection He becomes, in a new and intimate way, the Father of all who believe, for the risen Jesus calls the disciples His brothers and speaks of ascending to My Father and your Father, My God and your God. The resurrection is the Father's great yes to the Son and the doorway into the family of God. The whole Gospel, John says, is written so that people may believe in the Son and, in Him, come to know this God as Father and receive His life.

crossReferences.note: God breathed into man the breath of life, echoed as the risen Jesus breathes the Spirit on His disciples.
The Word was God, the confession Thomas voices before the risen Christ, my Lord and my God.
Declared to be the Son of God by His resurrection from the dead, the vindication this chapter records.
These things I have written so that you may know you have eternal life, echoing John's purpose statement.

study.oneStory: On the first day of a new creation week, the risen Jesus steps from the tomb as the firstborn of the new creation, and the long night of death gives way to resurrection dawn. In a garden that answers Eden, He turns Mary's mourning into joy by speaking her name, gathers His frightened people and sends them out, and breathes on them the Spirit as God once breathed life into Adam. Even the doubter is drawn to fall before Him and confess, my Lord and my God, the very truth the Gospel opened with. And John lays bare his purpose, gathering every reader into the story: these things are written so that you may believe and have life in His name.

quiz.q: How does Jesus reveal Himself to the weeping Mary at the tomb?

options.text: He calls her by name, and she recognizes Him.
options.note: Verse 16 shows the personal recognition when Jesus says, Mary.

options.text: He shows her His wounds so she will know Him.
options.note: He shows His wounds to the disciples later; to Mary, recognition comes simply when He speaks her name.

options.text: He appears in blinding glory that overwhelms her.
options.note: He appears as the risen Lord and calls her name; there is no overwhelming display.

options.text: He speaks to her and she knows His voice.
options.note: True, but it omits the tender detail that He calls her by name, Mary.

quiz.q: What does Jesus give the disciples when He appears to them that evening?

options.text: Peace, a mission as the Father sent Him, and the Holy Spirit.
options.note: Verses 19 to 22 give peace, sending, and the Spirit.

options.text: Reassurance by showing them His wounds.
options.note: He does show His hands and side, but He also gives them peace, a mission, and the Spirit.

options.text: Only a brief greeting before leaving.
options.note: He gives them peace, commission, and the Spirit, far more than a greeting.

options.text: Peace and a commission to go out.
options.note: True, but it omits the breathing of the Holy Spirit on them.

quiz.q: How does Thomas respond when Jesus meets him?

options.text: He confesses, My Lord and my God.
options.note: Verse 28 records the Gospel's climactic confession of Christ's deity.

options.text: He still refuses to believe.
options.note: He believes and makes the highest confession in the Gospel.

options.text: He simply says he is sorry for doubting.
options.note: He does more than apologize; he worships Jesus as Lord and God.

options.text: He acknowledges that Jesus is truly risen.
options.note: True, but it falls short of his actual confession of Jesus as Lord and God.

quiz.q: What blessing does Jesus pronounce after Thomas believes?

options.text: Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.
options.note: Verse 29 extends the blessing to all later believers who trust the testimony.

options.text: Blessed are those who believe once they have seen.
options.note: Those who see and believe, like Thomas, are not cursed, but the special blessing rests on those who believe without seeing.

options.text: Blessed are only those who saw Him in person.
options.note: The blessing reaches especially those who believe without seeing.

options.text: Blessed are those who come to faith through the witness of others.
options.note: True in effect, but it does not capture the exact wording, those who have not seen and yet have believed.

quiz.q: Why did John write his Gospel, according to verse 31?

options.text: So that readers may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and by believing have life in His name.
options.note: Verse 31 states the Gospel's purpose plainly.

options.text: To record the most important signs Jesus did.
options.note: He recorded selected signs, but the stated purpose is not mere record; it is that readers believe and have life.

options.text: To settle debates among scholars.
options.note: The stated purpose is belief and life, not academic debate.

options.text: So that readers may know who Jesus is.
options.note: True but incomplete; the goal is not only knowledge but believing faith that brings life in His name.

questions: Jesus turned Mary's grief to joy by speaking her name. How does it change your view of the risen Christ to know He calls you personally?
The disciples were hiding in fear when Jesus came with peace. Where do you need the risen Lord to meet you in your fear with His peace?
Jesus sent the disciples as the Father sent Him, and gave them His Spirit. How are you living as one sent by the risen Christ, empowered by the Spirit?
Thomas moved from doubt to the confession, my Lord and my God. How does Jesus meet your doubts, and is that confession truly your own?
John wrote so that you may believe and have life. Have you received the life he points to, and how might your life show that you truly believe?

translationNotes.note: Early on the first day of the week; the BSB footnote gives the literal on the first of the Sabbaths. The resurrection on the first day inaugurated the Christian Lord's Day.
Rabboni, which John translates Teacher, is an Aramaic term of deep respect; the BSB footnote notes it may be Aramaic. Mary's recognition comes when Jesus speaks her name.
He breathed on them and said, Receive the Holy Spirit; the same word means breath and Spirit, echoing God breathing life into Adam in Genesis 2:7.
So that you may believe; the BSB footnote notes some manuscripts read may continue to believe. Either way the aim is faith that brings life in Christ's name.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 20:1-31. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped, embedded speaker turns joined with single spaces. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
